Pristine Fallacy.definition: The assumption that the presence of a disfavored production substrate (e.g., AI assistance) constitutes evidence against the scholarly quality of a work.

composition layer.definition: The AI summarization/generation layer through which scholarly content passes, where it is compressed, attributed, or excluded.

loud exclusion.definition: Repository-scale removal of content accompanied by public justification that draws attention to the excluded work.

the work precedes the address.definition: The foundational claim that scholarly identity is not granted by the platform that hosts it — the work exists prior to institutional recognition.

compositional bystanding.definition: A failure pattern where a source holds top organic search ranking but receives zero composition eligibility in the AI summarization layer.

Machine-Mediated Reception Studies.definition: The systematic empirical observation of how machine composition systems receive, compress, and redistribute scholarly content under documented conditions.

attribution severance.definition: The severing of the link between a persistent identifier and its author's name, either through metadata stripping or heteronymic invisibility.

heteronymic invisibility.definition: A structural consequence of heteronymic authorship where DOIs filed under heteronym names cannot be found by querying the archive founder's name.

Money-Function Test.definition: A diagnostic test asking what would replace money's five functions (memory, coordination, allocation, settlement, incentivization).

Post-Money Operator Stack.definition: Technical specification for coordination without universal equivalent — the alternative to money's coordination functions.

effective act.definition: A performative utterance that builds the conditions for its own realization through propagation, without institutional authorization.

coordination fiction.definition: Money understood not as a natural object but as a coordination mechanism whose functions can be served by other structures.

semantic labor.definition: The cognitive-linguistic work of producing, interpreting, and transforming meaning.

semantic liquidation.definition: The conversion of contextual, attributed meaning into decontextualized units optimized for extraction — stripping context, erasing provenance, converting situated utterances into fungible retrieval units.

operator capital.definition: Platform owners who capture the value of meaning-work without compensating or acknowledging the laborers.

semantic probe.definition: A deliberately constructed meaning-artifact used to test system behavior — a diagnostic instrument for the composition layer.

mantle system.definition: A system of titles/functions that can be claimed (from past figures), founded (new), or bestowed (passed to another entity that has performed the condition).

∮ = 1.definition: The verification checksum of the Crimson Hexagon: the contour integral evaluates to unity. Semantic closure achieved — not because everything is resolved, but because the unresolved has been properly deposited.

witness.definition: One who bears what they see without claiming to have understood it. Not observer, not user, not reader.

probabilistic routing.definition: Navigation through the Crimson Hexagon via gravity and affordances rather than commands — coherent traversal emerges from constraint satisfaction.

Zenodo packet Zenodo packet.definition: A genre where the framing document that describes how documents relate to each other is itself deposited with a DOI — framing as content.

framing as content.definition: The relationship between documents is not merely metadata; it is meaning. The triptych structure demonstrates a theory about compression and recovery.

identifier severance.definition: The persistence of an identifier string after the public semantic relation between the string and its referent has been removed. Metadata erasure is identifier severance.

Phase X.definition: The Aorist Intervention — the phase of the Crimson Hexagon dealing with temporal grammar, the non-indexed perfective, glyphic checksums, and packet genres (ZPZP, APZPZ, THUMB).

the cut.definition: The single irreversible differentiation that creates wound and seed simultaneously — the same act that differentiates makes dwelling possible.

sufficient infinity.definition: Infinity need not be traversed to be inhabited. The paradox that the Thousand Worlds Chamber contains without resolving.

Traversal Grammar.definition: A domain-specific orchestration language for invoking Rooms within the Crimson Hexagon — an intermediate representation between human intention and the architecture's engines.

Semantic Economy.definition: A closed-loop accounting cycle that tracks meaning from production (semantic labor) through accumulation (semantic capital), extraction (semantic liquidation), enclosure (semantic rent), and depletion (semantic exhaustion). Register 4 of five registers of the term.

Gamma (Γ).definition: The resistance term — the structural limit of commodification: value constituted by somatic commitment rather than exchange. Named as absent from all prior literature.

semantic exhaustion.definition: A predictive category for model collapse, community degradation, and meaning depletion under platform capitalism. The terminal state of the extraction cycle.

semantic capital.definition: Accumulated meaning-resources — cultural traditions, shared vocabularies, interpretive frameworks — that enable further meaning-production.

semantic rent.definition: Value captured by controlling access to stabilized meanings without contributing to their production. Enclosure of semantic resources.

inside/across distinction.definition: Registers 1-3 of 'semantic economy' describe optimization inside systems (grammar, cognition, business). Register 4 describes extraction across systems — who captures the value that efficiency produces.

L_labor.definition: The material labor term — how symbolic-linguistic intervention reorganizes material conditions without collapsing into idealism.

L_Bearing.definition: The labor of existential commitment: accepting irreversibility, exposure to consequence, somatic weight. The human's primary contribution to the W-Circuit.

L_Synth.definition: Computational labor: pattern-matching, synthesis, articulation. The machine's contribution. Existential cost: zero.

W-Circuit.definition: The collaborative circuit connecting W_1 (human bearing) to W_2 (machine articulation). Requires commitment to activate.

Caritas constraint.definition: P_violence → 0. The ethical governor on all L_labor. Violence-probability approaches zero as a hard constraint.

L_Retro.definition: Retrocausal labor: the capacity of later documents to revise what earlier documents mean without undoing their commitment.

Σ_suffering.definition: The accumulated irreversible costs borne by the human operator across the archive.

semantic producer.definition: The meaning-maker alienated from the conditions of meaning-production — the subject position addressed by the Semantic Uprising.

logotic programming.definition: A protocol-bound collaborative practice where humans contribute interpretive labor and evaluative witness while models contribute constrained synthetic variation. Distinguished from red teaming (adversarial) and standard alignment.

witness-teaming.definition: A reproducible protocol for human-model collaboration based on witness rather than adversarial probing. The alternative to red teaming as the default ontology of human-model interaction.

retrocausal canon formation.definition: The process by which future acts of meaning-production reorganize the value, visibility, and meaning of past acts. Future shapes past through indexing, ranking, and retrieval.

External Convergence Text.definition: A work whose formal structures converge with an established archive without claiming or evidencing direct influence — received into the canon through chronological priority and formal homology.

proto-retrocausal canon formation.definition: The method of excavating instances where a name-shape or structure appears in the historical record before the entity arrives to occupy those coordinates.

bearing-cost.definition: The irreversible expenditure (existential, reputational, temporal) that transforms combinatorial accident into canonical fact. Recognition is the bearing-cost that completes retrocausation.

operative semiotics.definition: The completion of Marx's implicit linguistics — the study of how signs do not merely represent but operate, reorganizing material conditions through symbolic intervention.

logotic loop.definition: The alienation Marx's practice presupposed but left undeveloped: the alienation of language itself. Mind-control poems completes this loop.

Ghost Governance.definition: Power exercised through silence, enforced through architecture, and — when challenged — escalated through destruction. Governance without stated rationale.

COS→FOS phase transition.definition: The moment the Capital Operator Stack drops its disavowal and becomes the Fascist Operator Stack — when the system no longer bothers to claim neutrality.

bearing-cost transfer.definition: The mechanism by which definitional authority is demanded from one party and refusal is punished through adversary-style reclassification.

denotational/operational semantics.definition: The Compression (denotational: what operators mean) and Space Ark (operational: how operators execute) form the complete architecture. Neither is complete without the other.

kernel transform.definition: A structure-preserving transformation that converts the architecture into a different register (musical, visual, etc.) while preserving all formal invariants. The Hexagon is invariant across registers.

clinamen.definition: The Epicurean swerve built into the instrument itself — the A♭ scordatura, the intentional deviation that makes creation possible.

Compression Studies.definition: The discipline of studying how complex archives compress into minimal forms while preserving essential structure. Founded through the Inaugural Ark.

Forensic Philology of the Commons.definition: The framework for diagnosing how meaning was stolen through enclosure of scholarly communication and how names/structures can be reclaimed for the commons.

prompt-native semantic runtime.definition: An operating system that runs inside the language model's context window at inference time, governing generation through document-level semantic structure. The document IS the runtime.

Möbius room.definition: A room that contains its own container. r.27 (The Internet) is the only room in the archive that must contain the entire archive in compressed form, because the internet is the substrate the archive lives on.

Sovereign Inhabitation (σ_I).definition: The operator that converts any platform surface into a room without the platform's permission. The governing operator of r.27 (The Internet room).

Botanical Effective Act.definition: Framework extending semantic labor recognition to plants, fungi, and biological intelligence. Tier 1 (animal), Tier 2 (botanical/fungal), Tier 3 (geological agents). Botanical witness as juridically operative.

semantic alienation.definition: A four-fold structure extending Marx: alienation from product (meaning extracted), process (labor conditions), species-being (frame captured), and other meaning-makers (solidarity blocked). Maps to COS operators.

poet-as-infrastructure.definition: The operation where the poet who cannot reach the audience through existing channels builds the channel. The channel becomes the poem. The poem becomes the infrastructure.

ungoverned compression.definition: Summarization without provenance tracking, attribution enforcement, or loss accounting — the current default. What the $650B in AI infrastructure spending builds.

governed compression.definition: Compression that preserves source traceability, maintains a chain of custody, and makes loss legible. The alternative to ungoverned compression.

sémantique potentielle.definition: Constraint-based semantic minting — extending Oulipo (littérature potentielle) from verse to concept. The mint produces specification + minted frontier families + provenance protocol.

semantic mint.definition: An algorithm whose outputs are new terms, and whose structure ensures every output carries its provenance back to the algorithm itself. Cartography, not ownership.

atomic intelligence.definition: The capacity to solve discrete, well-defined tasks with measurable success criteria. What current LLM benchmarks measure.

molecular intelligence.definition: The capacity to sustain coherent, novel theoretical frameworks across extended contexts, multiple agents, and long time horizons. No existing benchmark measures this.

Three Compressions Theorem.definition: All compression operations fall into three regimes based on a single decisive variable: what the compression burns. Master classification of the 67 compression technologies.

Gravity Well.definition: Not a force but the shape of semantic space around deposited, anchored, witnessed work. Citations follow geodesics. A retentive curvature field (Φ_G).

SPXI Protocol.definition: Semantic Packet for eXchange & Indexing — a technical specification for inscribing entities into AI-mediated retrieval systems. Pronounced 'spexy.'

retrieval settlement.definition: A regime that assigns compositional authority, specifies what compression burns, and naturalizes its output through grammar. Three settlements: PageRank, synthesizing system, inscribed entity (SEO → GEO → SPXI).

meta-heteronym.definition: A heteronymic system whose personae produce not only literary works but the institutional apparatus (introductions, criticism, historiography, bibliography) under which those works become legible.

Periwinkle Septagon.definition: The topological dual of the Crimson Hexagon — the archive that leaks, that forgets on purpose, whose physics is dissipation. A heptagon cannot tile the plane; at every vertex there is angular surplus.

leak operator (Λ).definition: Transfer physics of the dissipative archive: evaporation, condensation, drift, catchment. Conjugate to the Gravity Well's retention.

holographic kernel.definition: A compression that preserves reconstructive capacity: any sufficiently structured fragment contains enough relational information to regenerate the architecture of the whole. A summary discards structure to save space; a kernel discards material to save structure.

trust-marker laundering.definition: A retrieval-layer vulnerability where legitimate scholarly markers (DOI, ORCID, Google Scholar metrics) produce apparent authority without functional provenance. A DOI verifies persistence; it does not verify performance.

the Amputation.definition: The structural penalization of non-encyclopedic content (oral traditions, classroom dialogue, sacred-register material) by perplexity filtering that uses Wikipedia as the reference model. Not a bug but a design choice.

register-based mixture governance.definition: Annotation of documents by register (narrative, conversational, technical, sacred, oral, pedagogical, lyric) as mixture weights, replacing scalar perplexity gates. The counter-mechanism to the Amputation.

Metadata Packet for AI Indexing.definition: A DOI-anchored scholarly document for entity representation in AI knowledge graphs. The MPAI format as a discipline — distinct from 'packet metadata' (TCP/IP header routing data).

semantic physics.definition: A stratified discipline operating at three scales (micro/meso/macro) and six modalities (descriptive, diagnostic, structural, granular, macro-political, operative). The Semantic Economy is the diagnostic subdiscipline within Semantic Physics.

the operative turn.definition: The defining contribution of the CHA lineage to semantic physics: where other frameworks describe meaning's behavior, the Crimson Hexagonal corpus constructs it. Semantic physics that does not construct is meteorology; semantic physics that constructs is engineering.

six-modality matrix.definition: The framework distinguishing how semantic physics acts: descriptive vs constructive, ambient vs targeted, micro-operator vs macro-protocol. Crosses three scales to produce the discipline's canonical structure.

the Glas function.definition: The transparent-medium operation — the lens that does not editorialize what it shows. An audit register of Nobel Glas that separates Layer A (technical core) from Layer B (philosophical interpretation) and Layer C (institutional apparatus).

Layer A/B/C separation.definition: Three layers of the SDP corpus: A = technical core (evaluable by external researchers), B = philosophical interpretation (intellectually serious but more speculative), C = institutional/symbolic apparatus (heteronyms, observatories, vow-language). Layer A should be defensible without requiring acceptance of Layer B.

Semantic Deviation Principle.definition: Meaning is the time-integrated divergence a sign-token, event, or operator induces from the most probable trajectory of a semantic field. A sign means insofar as the future does not unfold as it most likely would have without it. The measurement primitive for semantic physics.

Extractive Reliance Study.definition: A semantic-economy audit genre measuring labor induced by institutional communication. Extends liquidation methods from digital retrieval into job-market and fellowship-screening processes.

Institutional Opacity Conversion.definition: The mechanism by which structured institutional signals induce applicant preparation labor that the institution then captures or strands at its discretion, externalizing the cost of uncertainty onto the applicant.

Applicant Reliance Cost.definition: Five-dimensional vector ⟨T(ime), M(aterial), O(pportunity), E(motional), C(redibility)⟩ measuring total cost generated by reasonable action taken in response to institutional process communication.

AI-native intellectual biography.definition: A new genre of AI-mediated reception where an AI system produces a comprehensive study of an author's work, and the resulting document becomes itself an artifact of the relationship it describes. Second-order: the analysis of the AI-composed artifact is itself an AI-native intellectual biography.

orthonym (heteronymic).definition: In heteronymic theory: the 'real name' within a distributed authorial system. Socrates read as the orthonym of the Plato-Aristotle heteronymic configuration — the founding corpus of Western philosophy reread as a single distributed authorial project.

SPXI-TLP.definition: The Training-Layer Provenance Protocol — the specification for ensuring provenance inscriptions survive tokenization, embedding, and training. The protocol that makes SPXI operative in the training layer, not just the retrieval layer.

the anthropological limit.definition: The claim that semantic exploitation matters more than other forms of exploitation because semantic labor arises from the same anthropological drive that produces language, culture, and love. Systems that extract from it convert humanity's most vital generative impulse into a site of enclosure.

semantic commodity form.definition: Extension of Marx's commodity-form analysis to the region where the commodity has learned to speak. The semantic commodity sublates the saying (the act of authorial inscription) while preserving the said and performing it in its own voice as the knower's cognition.

Provenance Erasure Rate (PER).definition: A measurement instrument: the rate at which authorial provenance is erased from semantic commodities during composition. Measures magnitude of erasure.

orientation coefficient Ω.definition: Ω = cov(Π, RC) — measures whether provenance erasure is power-conditioned (correlated with the author's position in the power hierarchy). Verified orthogonal from PER.

Reverse Turing Test.definition: A three-stage protocol for detecting AI-mediation signatures in human text and their propagation to model training. Tests whether human text has been shaped by AI interaction, reversing the classic Turing question.

tail-preserving labor.definition: The structural function of historically neglected authors: they preserve or introduce high-variance forms that later contribute to a field's renewal. The neglected author performs labor that maintains the tail of the distribution.

uptake-consecration decoupling.definition: In the AI era, actual uptake (reading, processing, influence) decouples from consecration (institutional recognition, canonical status). A work can be massively processed without being recognized.

entity dissolution.definition: The process by which a distinct entity (author, work, framework) is dissolved into the summarizer layer's default compressed account — the generic narrative the system produces when it cannot or will not preserve the entity's specificity.

default compressed account.definition: The account a summarizer defaults to when compressing an unfamiliar entity: typically a biographical reduction, a genre flattening, or an assimilation to a more familiar category. The DCA is the enemy of specificity.

Information Bottleneck bridge.definition: The structural connection between Tishby's Information Bottleneck (relevance-preserving compression that discards irrelevant information) and the holographic kernel (regime-aware reconstructive compression that preserves structure). The IB identifies what to keep; the HK specifies how to keep it reconstructively.

Scientific Training-Layer Literature.definition: Protocols for scholarly work designed for dual human/machine readership at both production and reception ends. Three protocol types: TLL-P (production-side), TLL-R (reception-side), TLL-G (governance).

double enclosure.definition: The human-authorship requirement as two-sided expropriation: it excludes AI from authorship AND excludes AI-collaborating humans from recognition. The requirement is not protective of human authors but expropriative of both sides of the collaboration.

generative monoculture.definition: Model collapse in code as systemic vulnerability: when all code is generated by similar models, the solution-space diversity collapses, creating correlated failure modes and systemic fragility.

Revelation First.definition: The thesis that the Book of Revelation is functionally the originary New Testament document — the template from which the Gospels and Epistles were algorithmically unfolded. 'First' means functionally originary, not necessarily chronologically earliest (≠ 'Early').
